context: The Hubble Space Telescope was launched by NASA in April 1990 aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery. It orbits Earth at an altitude of about 547 kilometers and has provided some of the most detailed images of distant galaxies. Hubble was named after the astronomer Edwin Hubble, who discovered that the universe is expanding. The telescope operates primarily in visible, ultraviolet, and near-infrared wavelengths. Over the decades, several servicing missions by astronauts extended its operational life.
questions: In what year was the Hubble Space Telescope launched?
Which Space Shuttle carried Hubble into orbit?
At approximately what altitude does Hubble orbit Earth?
Who was the telescope named after?

context: The Berlin Wall was constructed by the German Democratic Republic in August 1961. It physically and ideologically divided East Berlin from West Berlin during the Cold War. The wall stretched for about 155 kilometers around West Berlin. It remained a powerful symbol of political division until it was opened in November 1989. Its fall paved the way for the reunification of Germany in 1990.
questions: In which year was the Berlin Wall constructed?
Which government built the Berlin Wall?
Approximately how long was the wall around West Berlin?
In what year was the wall opened?

context: The Amazon River is the largest river by discharge volume in the world. It flows through several South American countries including Peru, Colombia, and Brazil. The river originates in the Andes Mountains in Peru and travels eastward across the continent. Its approximate length is about 6,400 kilometers. The Amazon eventually empties into the Atlantic Ocean along the northern coast of Brazil.
questions: Where does the Amazon River originate?
Which ocean does the Amazon empty into?
What is the approximate length of the Amazon River?
Name one country the Amazon flows through.

context: DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, carries the genetic instructions used in the growth and development of living organisms. It is structured as a double helix composed of two strands. Each strand is made up of nucleotides containing a sugar, phosphate group, and nitrogenous base. The four main bases in DNA are adenine, thymine, cytosine, and guanine. DNA is primarily located in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells.
questions: What does DNA stand for?
What shape is the structure of DNA?
Name one of the four bases found in DNA.
Where is DNA primarily located in eukaryotic cells?

context: The programming language Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. It emphasizes readability and uses indentation to define code blocks. Python supports multiple programming paradigms including procedural, object-oriented, and functional programming. It is widely used in fields such as data science, web development, and artificial intelligence. The Python Software Foundation currently maintains and oversees the language.
questions: Who created Python?
In what year was Python first released?
What organization maintains Python?
Name one programming paradigm supported by Python.

context: Gross Domestic Product, commonly called GDP, measures the total value of goods and services produced within a country over a specific period. Economists often calculate GDP annually or quarterly. One common method for calculating GDP is the expenditure approach, which adds consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports. GDP is frequently used to compare economic performance between countries. However, it does not account for income inequality or environmental impacts.
questions: What does GDP measure?
How often is GDP commonly calculated?
Name one component included in the expenditure approach.
What major factor does GDP not account for according to the context?
answers: the total value of goods and services produced within a country

context: The Mona Lisa is a portrait painting created by Leonardo da Vinci during the early 16th century. It is believed to have been painted between 1503 and 1506, although some scholars think work continued later. The painting depicts a woman widely thought to be Lisa Gherardini. Today it is displayed in the Louvre Museum in Paris. The Mona Lisa is famous for the subject’s subtle smile and the painting’s atmospheric background.
questions: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
Between which years is the painting believed to have been created?
Where is the Mona Lisa displayed today?
Who is widely believed to be the subject of the painting?

context: Isaac Newton published the book Principia Mathematica in 1687. In this work, he described the three laws of motion that explain how objects move. The first law states that an object will remain at rest or move in a straight line unless acted upon by an external force. The second law relates force, mass, and acceleration. The third law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
questions: In what year was Principia Mathematica published?
How many laws of motion did Newton describe in the book?
What does the first law of motion say about an object at rest?
What relationship does the second law describe?
answers: it will remain at rest or move in a straight line unless acted upon by an external force

context: Water is a chemical compound composed of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. Its chemical formula is H2O. Under standard atmospheric pressure, water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius and boils at 100 degrees Celsius. Water is often called a universal solvent because it can dissolve many substances. It plays a critical role in biological processes and climate regulation.
questions: What is the chemical formula of water?
How many hydrogen atoms are in a molecule of water?
At what temperature does water freeze under standard pressure?
At what temperature does water boil under standard pressure?

context: Apollo 11 was the first mission to land humans on the Moon. It was launched by NASA on July 16, 1969, from Kennedy Space Center in Florida. The mission carried astronauts Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins. Armstrong and Aldrin landed on the lunar surface in the Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969. Neil Armstrong became the first human to step onto the Moon.
questions: Which mission first landed humans on the Moon?
On what date was Apollo 11 launched?
Name one astronaut on the Apollo 11 mission.
Which astronaut first stepped onto the Moon?

context: Mount Everest is the highest mountain above sea level on Earth. It is located in the Himalayas on the border between Nepal and China. The mountain has an official height of 8,848.86 meters according to a joint survey conducted in 2020. Everest is part of the Mahalangur Himal sub-range of the Himalayas. The first confirmed successful summit was achieved in 1953 by Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay.
questions: What is the official height of Mount Everest?
Between which two countries is Mount Everest located?
In which mountain range is Everest found?
Who achieved the first confirmed successful summit?

context: The Internet is a global network of interconnected computer networks that communicate using standardized protocols. One of the most important protocols is TCP/IP, which stands for Transmission Control Protocol and Internet Protocol. The modern Internet grew out of ARPANET, a research network developed by the United States Department of Defense in the late 1960s. Email became one of the earliest widely used Internet applications. Today billions of devices are connected to the Internet worldwide.
questions: What does TCP/IP stand for?
Which earlier network did the modern Internet grow out of?
Who developed ARPANET?
Name one early widely used Internet application.
answers: Transmission Control Protocol and Internet Protocol
the United States Department of Defense

context: The greenhouse effect is a natural process that warms the Earth’s surface. It occurs when gases such as carbon dioxide, methane, and water vapor trap heat in the atmosphere. These gases allow sunlight to enter the atmosphere but absorb some of the outgoing infrared radiation. Without the greenhouse effect, Earth would be much colder and less suitable for life. Human activities such as burning fossil fuels have increased greenhouse gas concentrations.
questions: Name one gas that contributes to the greenhouse effect.
What type of radiation do greenhouse gases absorb?
What natural process warms the Earth’s surface according to the context?
What human activity increases greenhouse gas concentrations?

context: William Shakespeare wrote the tragedy Romeo and Juliet in the late 16th century. The play tells the story of two young lovers from the feuding families of Montague and Capulet. It is set primarily in the Italian city of Verona. Romeo and Juliet secretly marry with the help of Friar Laurence. The play ends tragically with the deaths of both protagonists.
questions: Who wrote Romeo and Juliet?
In which city is the play primarily set?
What are the names of the two feuding families?
Who helps Romeo and Juliet marry?

context: Vaccines work by stimulating the immune system to recognize and fight pathogens. Many vaccines contain weakened or inactivated forms of viruses or bacteria. When the immune system encounters the vaccine, it produces antibodies without causing the disease itself. This process creates immunological memory. As a result, the body can respond more quickly if it encounters the real pathogen later.
questions: What do vaccines stimulate in the body?
What do many vaccines contain?
What does the immune system produce after encountering a vaccine?
What type of memory is created by vaccination?
answers: weakened or inactivated forms of viruses or bacteria

context: The first successful powered airplane flight was achieved by the Wright brothers in 1903. Orville Wright piloted the aircraft while Wilbur Wright assisted from the ground. The flight took place at Kitty Hawk in North Carolina. The aircraft used in the flight was known as the Wright Flyer. The first flight lasted about 12 seconds and covered a distance of 36 meters.
questions: In what year did the first powered airplane flight occur?
Who piloted the aircraft during the first flight?
Where did the flight take place?
What was the aircraft called?

context: Ludwig van Beethoven was a German composer and pianist of the late 18th and early 19th centuries. He was born in Bonn in 1770 and later moved to Vienna, where he spent much of his career. Beethoven composed nine symphonies, including the famous Ninth Symphony. The Ninth Symphony features the choral setting of Friedrich Schiller’s poem 'Ode to Joy'. Despite losing his hearing later in life, Beethoven continued composing.
questions: In which city was Beethoven born?
In what year was Beethoven born?
How many symphonies did Beethoven compose?
Which poem appears in the Ninth Symphony?

context: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The number 2 is the smallest prime number and also the only even prime number. Prime numbers play a key role in number theory. They are also used in modern cryptographic systems such as RSA encryption. The sequence of prime numbers begins with 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11.
questions: What is the smallest prime number?
Which prime number is the only even prime?
Name one field where prime numbers play a key role.
Which cryptographic system mentioned uses prime numbers?

context: The Eiffel Tower is a wrought-iron lattice tower located in Paris, France. It was designed by the engineer Gustave Eiffel and completed in 1889. The tower was built as the centerpiece of the 1889 World’s Fair. It stands about 330 meters tall including antennas. Today it is one of the most recognizable landmarks in the world.
questions: Who designed the Eiffel Tower?
In which year was the tower completed?
For which event was the Eiffel Tower built?
Approximately how tall is the Eiffel Tower?

context: The United Nations was established after World War II to promote international cooperation and prevent future conflicts. Its founding charter was signed on June 26, 1945, in San Francisco. The organization officially came into existence on October 24, 1945. The UN headquarters is located in New York City. Today the organization has 193 member states.
questions: When was the UN Charter signed?
In which city was the charter signed?
Where is the UN headquarters located?
How many member states does the United Nations have?

context: The Mariana Trench is the deepest known part of the world's oceans. It is located in the western Pacific Ocean east of the Mariana Islands. The deepest point of the trench is called Challenger Deep. Challenger Deep reaches a depth of about 10,984 meters below sea level. The trench was first measured during the HMS Challenger expedition in the 1870s.
questions: What is the deepest known part of the world's oceans?
Where is the Mariana Trench located?
What is the deepest point of the trench called?
Approximately how deep is Challenger Deep?

context: Hurricanes are powerful tropical storms that form over warm ocean waters. They are known as typhoons in the western Pacific and cyclones in the Indian Ocean. Hurricanes typically develop when sea surface temperatures exceed about 26.5 degrees Celsius. These storms can produce strong winds, heavy rainfall, and storm surges. Meteorologists classify hurricanes using the Saffir–Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale.
questions: Over what type of water do hurricanes form?
What are hurricanes called in the western Pacific?
At approximately what sea temperature do hurricanes typically develop?
What scale is used to classify hurricanes?

context: Bacteria are microscopic single-celled organisms that can be found in many environments. They were first observed by Antonie van Leeuwenhoek in the 17th century using early microscopes. Bacteria reproduce primarily through a process called binary fission. Some bacteria are beneficial and help with digestion in the human gut. Others can cause diseases such as tuberculosis and cholera.
questions: Who first observed bacteria?
In which century were bacteria first observed?
What process do bacteria primarily use to reproduce?
Name one disease caused by bacteria.

context: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built during the reign of the Egyptian Pharaoh Khufu. Construction likely took place around 2580 to 2560 BCE. The pyramid was originally about 146.6 meters tall. It remained the tallest man-made structure in the world for more than 3,800 years. The pyramid is part of the larger Giza pyramid complex near Cairo.
questions: Which pharaoh commissioned the Great Pyramid of Giza?
Around what years was the pyramid constructed?
What was the original height of the pyramid?
Near which city is the Giza pyramid complex located?

context: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert light energy into chemical energy. It takes place primarily in structures called chloroplasts within plant cells. During photosynthesis, plants use carbon dioxide and water to produce glucose and oxygen. Chlorophyll, the green pigment in plants, absorbs light energy from the sun. This process is essential for sustaining life on Earth.
questions: In which cell structure does photosynthesis mainly occur?
What pigment absorbs light energy during photosynthesis?
Which gas is used by plants in photosynthesis?
What sugar is produced during photosynthesis?

context: The Titanic was a British passenger liner that sank in the North Atlantic Ocean in April 1912. It struck an iceberg during its maiden voyage from Southampton to New York City. The ship was operated by the White Star Line. More than 1,500 passengers and crew lost their lives in the disaster. The wreck of the Titanic was discovered in 1985 by an expedition led by Robert Ballard.
questions: In what year did the Titanic sink?
What did the Titanic strike before sinking?
Which company operated the Titanic?
Who led the expedition that discovered the wreck?

context: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun in our solar system. It is often called the Red Planet because of iron oxide on its surface. Mars has two small moons named Phobos and Deimos. The largest volcano in the solar system, Olympus Mons, is located on Mars. A day on Mars lasts approximately 24.6 hours.
questions: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
How many moons does Mars have?
What is the name of the largest volcano on Mars?
How long is a day on Mars?

context: A central processing unit, or CPU, is the primary component of a computer responsible for executing instructions. It performs arithmetic, logic, and control operations. Modern CPUs often contain multiple cores that allow them to process tasks in parallel. The CPU works closely with memory such as RAM to retrieve and store data. Major CPU manufacturers include Intel and AMD.
questions: What does CPU stand for?
What types of operations does the CPU perform?
What allows modern CPUs to process tasks in parallel?
Name one major CPU manufacturer.

context: Dinosaurs first appeared during the Triassic period around 230 million years ago. They dominated terrestrial ecosystems during the Jurassic and Cretaceous periods. Most dinosaurs became extinct about 66 million years ago. This extinction event is widely linked to an asteroid impact near the Yucatán Peninsula. Birds are considered modern descendants of certain theropod dinosaurs.
questions: During which period did dinosaurs first appear?
Approximately how many million years ago did dinosaurs appear?
When did most dinosaurs become extinct?
What event is linked to their extinction?

context: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity using photovoltaic cells. These cells are commonly made from semiconductor materials such as silicon. When sunlight strikes the cells, it causes electrons to move and generate an electric current. Solar power is considered a renewable energy source because sunlight is naturally replenished. Many large solar farms are built in regions with high sunlight exposure.
questions: What type of cells do solar panels use to generate electricity?
What material is commonly used to make photovoltaic cells?
What movement generates electric current in solar cells?
Why is solar power considered renewable?

context: Lake Baikal is the deepest freshwater lake in the world. It is located in Siberia, Russia. The lake reaches a maximum depth of about 1,642 meters. Lake Baikal contains roughly 20 percent of the world’s unfrozen freshwater. It is also one of the oldest lakes on Earth, estimated to be about 25 million years old.
questions: Where is Lake Baikal located?
What is the maximum depth of Lake Baikal?
Approximately what percentage of the world’s unfrozen freshwater does it contain?
How old is Lake Baikal estimated to be?

context: Gregor Mendel is known as the father of modern genetics. In the 1860s, he conducted experiments on pea plants to study how traits are inherited. Mendel discovered that traits are passed from parents to offspring through discrete units called genes. He formulated the laws of segregation and independent assortment. His work laid the foundation for modern genetic science.
questions: Who is known as the father of modern genetics?
What type of plants did Mendel study?
What units carry inherited traits?
Name one law Mendel formulated.

context: Jet engines power most modern commercial aircraft. They operate by drawing in air, compressing it, mixing it with fuel, and igniting the mixture. The expanding gases are expelled at high speed to produce thrust. One common type of jet engine is the turbofan engine. Turbofan engines are widely used on passenger airliners because they are efficient and relatively quiet.
questions: What powers most modern commercial aircraft?
What is mixed with compressed air in a jet engine?
What produces thrust in a jet engine?
Name one type of jet engine used on passenger aircraft.
answers: expanding gases expelled at high speed

context: The Indus Valley Civilization flourished around 2600 to 1900 BCE in South Asia. Major cities included Harappa and Mohenjo-daro. The civilization is known for advanced urban planning with grid-like street layouts. Houses often had access to sophisticated drainage systems. Archaeologists have also found standardized weights and measures used for trade.
questions: When did the Indus Valley Civilization flourish?
Name one major city of the civilization.
What type of street layout did the cities have?
What system existed in many houses for waste management?

context: The human immune system defends the body against pathogens such as bacteria and viruses. White blood cells play a crucial role in identifying and destroying these invaders. One important type of white blood cell is the lymphocyte. Lymphocytes include B cells and T cells, which have specialized immune functions. B cells produce antibodies that bind to specific antigens.
questions: What type of cells play a key role in the immune response?
Name one type of white blood cell mentioned.
Which cells produce antibodies?
What do antibodies bind to?

context: The Colosseum is a large amphitheater located in Rome, Italy. It was commissioned by Emperor Vespasian of the Flavian dynasty. Construction began around 72 CE and was completed in 80 CE under Emperor Titus. The Colosseum could hold an estimated 50,000 spectators. It was used for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
questions: In which city is the Colosseum located?
Which emperor commissioned the Colosseum?
When was the Colosseum completed?
Approximately how many spectators could it hold?

context: A transistor is a semiconductor device used to amplify or switch electronic signals. It is one of the fundamental building blocks of modern electronic devices. Transistors are made from semiconductor materials such as silicon. They are widely used in integrated circuits found in computers and smartphones. The invention of the transistor in 1947 revolutionized electronics.
questions: What device is used to amplify or switch electronic signals?
What material is commonly used to make transistors?
Where are transistors widely used?
In what year was the transistor invented?

context: Rice is one of the most important staple crops in the world. It is a primary food source for more than half of the global population. Rice plants typically grow in flooded fields known as paddies. The crop thrives in warm climates with abundant water. Major rice-producing countries include China, India, and Indonesia.
questions: What type of crop is rice considered?
What are flooded rice fields called?
What climate conditions favor rice growth?
Name one major rice-producing country.

context: Socrates was a classical Greek philosopher credited as one of the founders of Western philosophy. He lived in Athens during the 5th century BCE. Socrates is known for the Socratic method, a form of dialogue that encourages critical thinking through questioning. He did not write any philosophical texts himself. Much of what is known about his ideas comes from the writings of his student Plato.
questions: In which city did Socrates live?
What method of questioning is associated with Socrates?
Did Socrates write philosophical texts?
Which student documented many of Socrates' ideas?

context: The mean is a measure of central tendency commonly used in statistics. It is calculated by adding all values in a dataset and dividing by the number of values. Another measure of central tendency is the median, which represents the middle value when data is ordered. The mode is the value that appears most frequently in a dataset. These measures help summarize and understand numerical data.
questions: How is the mean calculated?
What does the median represent?
Which value appears most frequently in a dataset?
What type of statistical concept are mean, median, and mode?
answers: adding all values and dividing by the number of values
the middle value when data is ordered

context: El Niño is a climate pattern characterized by unusually warm ocean temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean. It occurs every two to seven years. El Niño can influence weather patterns around the world. Some regions experience heavier rainfall, while others may face drought. The phenomenon is part of a larger cycle called the El Niño–Southern Oscillation.
questions: What ocean region experiences unusually warm temperatures during El Niño?
How often does El Niño typically occur?
What global systems can El Niño influence?
What larger climate cycle is El Niño part of?
answers: the central and eastern Pacific Ocean

context: The International Space Station is a large space laboratory orbiting Earth. It is a collaborative project involving NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA. The station travels at an average altitude of about 400 kilometers above Earth. Astronauts aboard the ISS conduct scientific experiments in microgravity. The first module of the station was launched in 1998.
questions: What space laboratory orbits Earth?
Name one agency involved in the ISS project.
At approximately what altitude does the ISS orbit?
When was the first module launched?

context: The English language belongs to the Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family. It developed from the dialects brought to Britain by Anglo-Saxon settlers. Over time, English absorbed vocabulary from languages such as Latin and French. The Norman Conquest of 1066 significantly influenced the English vocabulary. Today English is widely used as an international lingua franca.
questions: To which language family does English belong?
Who brought early forms of English to Britain?
Which conquest influenced English vocabulary in 1066?
What role does English play internationally today?

context: Vitamin C is an essential nutrient for humans. It plays an important role in the production of collagen. Vitamin C is also known as ascorbic acid. Citrus fruits such as oranges and lemons are rich sources of this vitamin. A deficiency of vitamin C can lead to the disease scurvy.
questions: What is another name for vitamin C?
What protein production does vitamin C support?
Name one fruit rich in vitamin C.
What disease results from vitamin C deficiency?

context: Robotics is a field that combines engineering, computer science, and artificial intelligence. Robots are machines designed to perform tasks automatically. Many robots use sensors to perceive their environment. These sensors can include cameras, lidar, and infrared detectors. Industrial robots are widely used in manufacturing for tasks such as welding and assembly.
questions: Which fields combine to form robotics?
What are robots designed to do?
Name one type of sensor used by robots.
In which industry are robots widely used for welding and assembly?
answers: engineering, computer science, and artificial intelligence

context: An ecosystem consists of living organisms interacting with their physical environment. These organisms include plants, animals, fungi, and microorganisms. Energy typically enters ecosystems through sunlight captured by plants. Plants convert this energy into chemical energy through photosynthesis. Nutrients cycle through ecosystems via processes such as decomposition.
questions: What interacts within an ecosystem?
How does energy typically enter ecosystems?
Which organisms capture sunlight in ecosystems?
Name one process involved in nutrient cycling.
answers: living organisms and their physical environment

context: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late 18th century. It marked a transition from manual labor to machine-based manufacturing. Key industries included textiles, iron production, and coal mining. Steam engines played a major role in powering factories and transportation. The revolution eventually spread to other parts of Europe and North America.
questions: Where did the Industrial Revolution begin?
During which century did it begin?
Name one key industry of the Industrial Revolution.
What technology powered many factories?

context: Earthquakes occur when energy stored in Earth's crust is suddenly released. This release creates seismic waves that travel through the ground. The point inside Earth where the earthquake begins is called the focus. The point directly above it on the surface is known as the epicenter. Scientists measure earthquake magnitude using scales such as the moment magnitude scale.
questions: What causes earthquakes?
What waves travel through the ground after an earthquake begins?
What is the underground starting point of an earthquake called?
What scale is used to measure earthquake magnitude?
answers: sudden release of energy in Earth's crust

context: Steel is an alloy primarily composed of iron and carbon. It is stronger and more durable than pure iron. The carbon content in steel typically ranges up to about 2 percent. Steel is widely used in construction, transportation, and infrastructure. Different types of steel are produced by varying alloying elements and heat treatments.
questions: What elements primarily compose steel?
How does steel compare to pure iron?
What is the typical maximum carbon percentage in steel?
Name one industry where steel is widely used.

context: Cloud computing allows users to access computing resources over the internet instead of relying on local hardware. Major cloud providers include Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud. Cloud services are commonly divided into three main models: Infrastructure as a Service, Platform as a Service, and Software as a Service. These services allow businesses to scale resources dynamically. Cloud computing has become central to modern web applications and data storage.
questions: What does cloud computing allow users to access over the internet?
Name one major cloud provider.
What are the three main cloud service models mentioned?
What capability do cloud services provide for businesses?
answers: Infrastructure as a Service, Platform as a Service, and Software as a Service

context: Homo sapiens are the only surviving species of the genus Homo. Fossil evidence suggests that modern humans first appeared in Africa around 300,000 years ago. Early Homo sapiens developed complex tools and symbolic communication. Over time they migrated out of Africa and spread across the globe. Archaeological discoveries such as cave paintings provide insight into their culture.
questions: Which species is the only surviving member of the genus Homo?
Where did modern humans first appear?
Approximately how many years ago did Homo sapiens appear?
What type of archaeological discovery reveals aspects of early human culture?

context: The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the Earth's surface. Key processes include evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and runoff. Evaporation occurs when liquid water changes into water vapor due to heat. Condensation forms clouds as water vapor cools and becomes liquid droplets. Precipitation returns water to the surface as rain, snow, or hail.
questions: What cycle describes the movement of water on Earth?
Name one process in the water cycle.
What process turns liquid water into vapor?
What process forms clouds?

context: Democracy is a system of government in which power ultimately resides with the people. Citizens typically exercise this power through voting in elections. Democracies often include institutions such as parliaments or legislatures. These bodies are responsible for making laws. Many democratic systems also include independent courts to interpret laws.
questions: Where does power ultimately reside in a democracy?
How do citizens commonly exercise political power?
What institutions often make laws in democratic systems?
Which institutions interpret laws?

context: Nanotechnology involves manipulating matter at the scale of nanometers. One nanometer is equal to one billionth of a meter. Materials at the nanoscale can exhibit unique physical and chemical properties. Applications of nanotechnology include medicine, electronics, and materials science. For example, nanoparticles are used in targeted drug delivery systems.
questions: What scale does nanotechnology manipulate matter at?
How long is one nanometer?
Name one field where nanotechnology is applied.
What medical application of nanoparticles is mentioned?

context: Nicolaus Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model of the solar system in the 16th century. In this model, the Sun rather than the Earth is at the center of the solar system. His ideas were published in the book De revolutionibus orbium coelestium in 1543. The heliocentric theory challenged the earlier geocentric model. Copernicus's work later influenced astronomers such as Galileo and Kepler.
questions: Who proposed the heliocentric model?
What object is at the center in the heliocentric model?
In what year was Copernicus's book published?
Name one astronomer influenced by Copernicus.

context: Coral reefs are diverse underwater ecosystems formed by colonies of coral polyps. These polyps secrete calcium carbonate to build hard skeletons. Coral reefs are commonly found in warm, shallow tropical waters. They support a vast variety of marine species including fish, mollusks, and crustaceans. The Great Barrier Reef off the coast of Australia is the largest coral reef system in the world.
questions: What organisms form coral reefs?
What substance do coral polyps secrete?
In what type of waters are coral reefs commonly found?
What is the largest coral reef system mentioned?

context: The first law of thermodynamics states that energy cannot be created or destroyed. Instead, energy can only be transferred or transformed from one form to another. This principle is often called the law of energy conservation. It applies to both physical and chemical processes. For example, chemical energy in fuel can be converted into heat and mechanical work.
questions: What does the first law of thermodynamics state about energy?
What is another name for the first law of thermodynamics?
In what types of processes does the law apply?
Into what forms can chemical energy in fuel be converted?
answers: energy cannot be created or destroyed

context: A map projection is a method used to represent the curved surface of the Earth on a flat map. Because Earth is spherical, any projection introduces some distortion. Different projections preserve different properties such as area, shape, or distance. The Mercator projection is widely used for navigation. However, it significantly distorts the size of landmasses near the poles.
questions: What does a map projection represent?
Why do map projections cause distortion?
Name one property that projections may preserve.
Which projection is widely used for navigation?
answers: the curved surface of the Earth on a flat map

context: Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in modern portable electronics. They power devices such as smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles. These batteries store energy through the movement of lithium ions between electrodes. One electrode is typically made of graphite while the other contains a lithium metal oxide. Lithium-ion batteries are valued for their high energy density.
questions: What type of batteries are commonly used in portable electronics?
Name one device powered by lithium-ion batteries.
What particles move between electrodes in these batteries?
What material is commonly used for one electrode?

context: The Olympic Games originated in ancient Greece. The first recorded Olympic Games were held in 776 BCE in the city of Olympia. Athletes from various Greek city-states competed in events such as running and wrestling. The games were held every four years. The modern Olympic Games were revived in 1896 in Athens.
questions: Where did the Olympic Games originate?
In what year were the first recorded Olympic Games held?
In which city were the ancient Olympics held?
When were the modern Olympic Games revived?
